variables.content: Average task success rates across the evaluated models increase monotonically as primitive abstraction increases from Low-Level tiers (S3/S4, where agents compose atomic perception and control primitives) to High-Level tiers (S1/S2, where agents call human-crafted macro primitives), indicating that providing human-designed high-level primitives that collapse perception, geometry, and control complexity substantially improves zero-shot programmatic control performance relative to operating directly over atomic interfaces.

The proposition is as follows. Define two agent classes: Thinking Agents are agent processes backed by high‑capacity, higher‑latency LLM instances intended for global codebase analysis, architectural planning, and deep review; Coding Agents are agent processes backed by lower‑latency, cost‑efficient LLM instances intended for iterative code generation and local test cycles. The claim is that assigning Thinking Agents to planning and review and assigning Coding Agents to implement isolated modules and execute local tests reduces the probability of (a) global mis‑specification arising from truncated context in lower‑latency Coding Agents, and (b) repeated costly remediation cycles, compared to using a single agent class for all tasks, thereby improving robustness of iterative development in large codebases.

Agentsway defines an explicit taxonomy of agent roles and their concrete responsibilities: a Planning Agent examines project documents and meeting artifacts to analyze requirements and decompose work into executable tasks and formal pitches; a Prompting Agent converts approved tasks into context‑aware prompts tailored for downstream code‑generation models; Coding Agents consume those prompts within sandboxed project environments and emit executable source code and accompanying documentation; Testing Agents run automated unit, integration, regression, static analysis, and vulnerability scans and emit structured defect reports and coverage statistics; and Fine‑Tuning Agents aggregate versioned prompts, code diffs, test outcomes, and human annotations after each cycle to drive incremental model updates.

Consider a centrally orchestrated multi-agent system in which a central orchestrator agent issues textual instructions to subordinate sub-agents (for example, WebSurfer or FileSurfer), and sub-agents execute actions via their own tool primitives. In this work, interventions are restricted to minimal edits at the orchestrator message level or to plan updates applied to orchestrator messages (no source-code changes to sub-agents and no addition of new tool primitives). The implicit working assumption is that a substantial portion of repairable failures can be corrected solely by such orchestrator-level textual edits; the weak point is that many failures may instead require direct sub-agent capability augmentation, code changes, or new tool primitives to be repairable.

A Scheduler component performs an initial pre‑analysis of submitted smart contract source code to extract syntactic and semantic features (including module boundaries, function call graph edges, external dependency identifiers, function signatures, and control‑flow markers); it queries the Knowledge Base using these extracted features to identify plausible vulnerability types for individual code regions based on pattern and contextual metadata similarity; it dispatches specialized Detection Agents to target code areas; it supports both parallel and sequenced execution of agents where some agents' outputs can guide others; it assigns resource and priority weights to agent tasks according to potential severity; it reconciles conflicting agent outputs; it invokes a General Agent as a fallback on code regions lacking sufficiently similar KB matches; and it aggregates agent outputs into a unified finding set with normalized severities and confidence scores.

Among the set of M = 14 generator programs that an automated rule‑based classifier marked as "ambiguous" because they exercise edge‑case DSL primitives (examples drawn from the corpus include subgrid(), crop(), switch()), it is feasible to produce a finite set of additional deterministic decision rules that (a) precisely detect the relevant edge‑case primitives by textual pattern or AST predicates in the generator source code, (b) map each previously ambiguous generator to one of the existing nine taxonomy categories, and (c) preserve or improve the classifier’s global accuracy, provided that each ambiguous generator’s semantic complexity reduces to detection of unhandled syntactic primitives rather than requiring introduction of genuinely new conceptual categories; this proposition presumes access to the ambiguous generators’ source code for rule construction.

The coding process used open inductive coding whenever an existing taxonomy did not capture an observed rejection reason, resulting in the addition of four new rejection categories intended to capture agent-specific behaviors; one named category is "Submission for experimentation" (abbreviated "Experimentation"), defined as a PR created to experiment with or evaluate a coding agent or development tool and not intended for merging, which in the 654-PR sample was observed at 2.9% for Agentic-PRs and at 0.9% for Human-PRs; these newly introduced categories were included alongside the prior taxonomy to describe failure modes that were not present in earlier single-agent or human-focused taxonomies.

Let "behavior taxonomy" denote the organized classification of reusable agent behaviors into the categories {creation-based, task-oriented, communication-oriented, mobility-related} and let "general-purpose" mean that in a majority of application domains where mobile agents are meaningfully employed (for example virtual learning environments, distributed monitoring, and mobile service delivery) the taxonomy's listed primitive patterns—Role Factory, Clone, Observer, Task, Listener, Client-Server, Itinerary—and the composition patterns Sequential, Parallel, and FSM provide either direct primitives or compositionally reachable primitives sufficient to express typical agent coordination and task patterns without introducing additional primitive behavior types; this proposition states that a taxonomy derived primarily from the VLE domain can be applied across those other domains as-is.

Applying axial coding to a combined corpus that includes (a) the four interview transcripts and the themes derived from those interviews and (b) an analyzed set of prior literature sources and workflow surveys (a collection of 43 multi-agent systems and additional cited surveys assembled through keyword searches and literature review), and grouping recurring structures and decision types into categories, yields the three distinct abstraction levels named Task Planning, Agent Assignment, and Agent Optimization; Task Planning denotes strategic decomposition into subtasks, Agent Assignment denotes mapping subtasks to agents or agent groups and selecting collaboration patterns, and Agent Optimization denotes per-agent prompt, tool, and retrieval configuration, and this coding process is reported as the method used to establish the hierarchical scaffold for tooling.

The architecture's taxonomy distinguishes team member agents, which are tied to individual team members and are responsible for updating the blackboard, and system agents, which act as integrators and support services, but the taxonomy does not specify how human socio-cognitive constraints (for example, awareness, trust, and conflict resolution), hand-off protocols between agents, or fine-grained coordination primitives and message schemas will be operationalized as agent behaviors, decision rules, or communication formats required for implementation with real human teams.

The proposition is that an environment API exposing four primitive classes—(A) virtualised time access and simulator-driven scheduling primitives, (B) simulator-controlled random-number generation primitives, (C) explicit perception-delivery and action-request primitives, and (D) abstract send/receive messaging primitives—is sufficient to ensure that a Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) agent program written exclusively against these primitives can execute unchanged in both a Discrete Event Simulation (DES) environment and in a real-world deployment. A BDI agent program here denotes a program that implements a repeated control loop with three phases (sense, deliberate, act) that (i) receives perceptions from the environment, (ii) updates internal beliefs, (iii) selects plans and intentions, and (iv) issues actions and messages. "Virtualised time access and scheduling primitives" are API calls that return a simulator-controlled notion of the current time and provide a mechanism to schedule timeouts or delays on the simulator event queue instead of reading an OS clock or invoking blocking sleep. "Controlled random-number generation primitives" are RNG functions whose output sequence is fully determined by a simulator-provided seed and simulator-managed RNG state. "Perception-delivery and action-request primitives" are API calls whereby the environment model delivers perception objects to agents and agents submit action descriptors to request environment mutations instead of performing direct hardware or system invocations. "Abstract send/receive messaging primitives" are transport-agnostic message API calls that hide transport-layer details and whose implementations can be provided either by a simulator message-delivery mechanism or by real networking stacks. The sufficiency claim applies only when the agent program confines all external interactions—time, randomness, perceptions, actions, and communication—to these API calls and does not use other platform-specific primitives (for example, raw OS sockets, direct hardware I/O, ad-hoc thread sleeps, or global singletons); under that confinement, providing platform-specific bindings for the four primitive classes suffices to port the same agent source between simulation and deployment without source-code changes.

Define an Optimization Agent O_A that performs automated static analysis on C# source fragments produced by a Coding Agent C_A, including (i) parsing method declarations labeled Method_New to extract explicit parameter and return type signatures, (ii) checking these signatures against the DLL registry for matching compiled artifacts, and (iii) verifying syntactic well‑formedness and simple semantic invariants (for example, absence of obvious null‑reference constructs). Such static analysis is sufficient, in many practical cases encountered in the reported experiments, to ensure that referenced methods are resolvable at compile time and to reduce a substantial fraction of compilation and linking failures, provided that C_A emits explicit type signatures, the registry contains compatible binaries, and the static checks cover the majority of errors that lead to compile‑time failures.

AutoSafeCoder is a multi-agent framework that structures automated Python code generation into three cooperating large-language-model (LLM) agents with explicit roles and an iterative feedback loop: (i) a Coding Agent, an LLM-based component that generates or repairs Python source code from natural-language requirements and partial function definitions using few-shot in-context prompts; (ii) a Static Analyzer Agent, an LLM-driven component that performs static vulnerability auditing by mapping findings to MITRE Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) identifiers and returning CWE-tagged remediation suggestions; and (iii) a Fuzzing Agent that performs execution-driven testing by (a) using an LLM-based Initial Seed Generator to produce meaningful initial input seeds and (b) applying a type-aware mutation strategy to those seeds to produce runtime inputs. In the AutoSafeCoder workflow the Coding Agent first produces code; the Static Analyzer Agent then reviews the code and exchanges vulnerability findings and remediation suggestions with the Coding Agent in an iterative loop for up to four rounds or until the Static Analyzer Agent declares the code "Safe" (i.e., reports no detected CWE findings); finally the code judged "Safe" by the static analysis is passed to the Fuzzing Agent, which executes the code in a sandboxed separate process, reports crashing inputs and error contexts back to the Coding Agent for targeted repair, and the loop continues until no vulnerabilities are reported or a terminal condition (four static-analysis rounds or other stop criterion) is reached.

A deployment-agnostic environment application programming interface (API) that lets the same Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) agent program run unchanged in both a Discrete Event Simulation (DES) incarnation and in a real-world deployment must encapsulate every platform-specific source of non-determinism and device-dependent service used by agent code. In this context, a BDI agent program is a program implementing a repeated control loop with three phases—sense, deliberate, act—whose external interactions are (i) reading time and scheduling timeouts or delays, (ii) drawing random values, (iii) receiving percepts and submitting action requests that mutate the environment, and (iv) sending and receiving messages to other agents. The minimal API surface sufficient for portability comprises four concrete primitive classes: (A) Time primitives: calls that return a simulator-controlled current time value and primitives to schedule timeouts or callbacks on the simulator-managed event queue so that agent code does not read operating-system clocks or invoke blocking sleep; (B) Randomness primitives: random-number generation functions whose internal state and seed are provided and controlled by the simulator runtime so that the simulator can deterministically reproduce pseudo-random sequences; (C) Perception-and-action primitives: an explicit receive-percept primitive that delivers perception objects produced or forwarded by the environment model, and an action-request primitive that submits action descriptors to the environment rather than performing direct hardware or system calls; (D) Communication primitives: abstract send/receive calls that present a transport-agnostic message API to agents and whose implementations may either perform real networking in a deployment or simulator-mediated delivery that can model delay, loss, and topology in a simulated environment. When all external interactions in the agent code are restricted to these four primitive classes, different interpreter implementations (one wired to a DES and one wired to real hardware and OS services) can provide alternative, semantically equivalent bindings for those primitives so that the same agent source code executes unchanged in both contexts.

METAPI exposes a deliberately extensive set of directly invocable primitives grouped as lexical primitives (operations that recognize input character sequences such as detecting a quoted string or a run of blank characters), syntactic primitives (operations that test token or symbol patterns such as detecting an integer form not followed by a period to distinguish integer from floating-point tokens), and semantic primitives (operations that emit target-language code templates such as generating Do-loop code patterns); some primitives are intentionally redundant (for example, a dedicated Do-loop primitive in addition to more general facilities) because these redundancies reduce METAPI description length and semantic-action complexity and yield implementation efficiencies that are difficult to obtain by composing more general primitives.
